examples.q: A book rests on a table. The table pushes upward on the book with a force equal to the book's weight. What type of force is this?
examples.solution:
This is the NORMAL FORCE.

Definition:
  The normal force (FN) is the contact force exerted
  by a surface on an object resting on it.
  It acts PERPENDICULAR to the surface.

Key facts:
  → It is a reaction force — it only exists when
    an object is in contact with a surface.
  → It balances the component of weight pushing
    into the surface.
  → On a flat surface, FN = weight of the object.

In this case:
  Weight of book pushes DOWN on table.
  Table pushes UP on book with equal force.
  That upward push is the Normal Force.

examples.q: A box is being pulled across a rough floor with a rope. The floor resists the motion of the box. What force does the floor exert on the box?
examples.solution:
This is FRICTION.

Definition:
  Friction is a contact force that opposes the
  relative motion (or tendency of motion) between
  two surfaces in contact.

Two main types:
  → Static friction: prevents an object from starting
    to move. Acts when the object is at rest.
  → Kinetic friction: acts on a moving object,
    opposing its motion.

In this case:
  The box is moving → kinetic friction applies.
  The floor exerts a friction force OPPOSITE to
  the direction of the box's motion.

examples.q: A chandelier hangs from the ceiling by a wire. What force does the wire exert on the chandelier?
examples.solution:
This is TENSION.

Definition:
  Tension is the pulling force transmitted through
  a string, rope, cable, or wire when it is pulled
  tight by forces acting from opposite ends.

Key facts:
  → Tension acts ALONG the rope or string.
  → It pulls the object toward the attachment point.
  → The rope pulls the chandelier UP and pulls
    the ceiling DOWN with equal magnitude.

In this case:
  The wire is taut because the chandelier's weight
  pulls it down. The wire pulls the chandelier UP —
  this upward pull is the tension force.

questions.q: Which force always acts downward toward the center of the Earth on any object with mass?
questions.given:
This force acts on ALL objects with mass.
It is always directed downward (toward Earth's center).
Formula: Fg = mg
questions.sol: Gravity (weight) is the force of Earth's gravitational pull on any mass. Fg = mg, always downward.

questions.q: A book is sitting still on a table. The table pushes up on the book. What is this force called?
questions.given:
This force is a contact force.
It acts perpendicular to the surface.
It balances the weight of the object on flat ground.
questions.sol: The normal force is the perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object resting on it.

questions.q: You push a heavy crate across the floor. The floor resists your push and slows the crate. What force is acting against the motion?
questions.given:
This force opposes motion between two surfaces.
It acts in the OPPOSITE direction of movement.
questions.sol: Kinetic friction opposes the motion of a sliding object. Since the crate is moving, kinetic friction applies.

questions.q: A person tries to push a heavy refrigerator but it does not move. What force prevents the refrigerator from moving?
questions.given:
This type of friction acts when an object is at REST.
It prevents the object from beginning to move.
questions.sol: Static friction acts when an object is at rest and prevents it from starting to move.

questions.q: A bucket is lifted by a rope. What is the force that the rope exerts on the bucket called?
questions.given:
This force is transmitted through a rope, cable, or string.
It acts along the rope, pulling the object toward the attachment point.
questions.sol: Tension is the pulling force transmitted through a rope or string. It acts upward along the rope on the bucket.

questions.q: Which of the following best defines 'applied force'?
questions.given:
An applied force is one that a person or object directly
exerts on another object.
opts: A force directly exerted on an object by a person or another object
The force of gravity on an object
The force a surface exerts perpendicular to an object
The resistance force between two surfaces
questions.sol: Applied force is a force directly exerted on an object by a person or another object, such as a push or pull.

questions.q: A spring is compressed and then pushes a toy car forward. What type of force does the spring exert?
questions.given:
This force is stored in elastic objects (springs, rubber bands).
It acts to restore the object to its natural shape.
Formula: F = kx  (Hooke's Law)
questions.sol: The spring force (elastic force) is stored energy in a compressed or stretched spring, described by Hooke's Law: F = kx.

questions.q: An object is falling through the air and a force opposes its downward motion. What is this force?
questions.given:
This force acts on objects moving through a fluid (air or liquid).
It opposes the direction of motion.
questions.sol: Air resistance (drag) is a force that opposes the motion of an object moving through air or any fluid.

questions.q: Two magnets attract each other without touching. What type of force is this an example of?
questions.given:
This force acts between objects without direct contact.
It can be attractive or repulsive.
It is a non-contact force.
questions.sol: Magnetic force is a non-contact force that acts between magnets or magnetic materials. No physical contact is needed.

questions.q: Which pair correctly matches the force type with its definition?
questions.given:
Review all force definitions:
Normal = perpendicular contact force from a surface
Friction = opposes motion between surfaces
Tension = pull through a rope
Gravity = pull toward Earth's center
opts: Friction — opposes motion between two surfaces in contact
Normal Force — pulls objects toward Earth's center
Gravity — acts perpendicular to a surface
questions.sol: Friction opposes relative motion between surfaces. The other options describe the wrong forces.
